name: Exam prep playbook (chat-app scrape → study guide)
description: Replayable workflow at ~/stats-exam-prep/PLAYBOOK.md for turning trapped-in-Gemini lecture notes + HWs + formula sheet into a targeted study guide under deadline
type: reference
originSessionId: 6071b181-5913-4d02-96b2-ec81bf3202f1
---
Full playbook at `~/stats-exam-prep/PLAYBOOK.md`. Covers: Playwright scraper (Gemini DOM selectors, login detection, sidebar-reorder pitfall, real-title extraction from page header), two-pass classify/reparse pipeline with weighted regex scoring, scope-hypothesis doc structure, and 9-section study guide template.

Reusable scripts live in `~/stats-exam-prep/scripts/`:
- `gemini_probe.py` — DOM diagnostic (run first when selectors break)
- `gemini_scrape.py` — main scraper with persistent Chromium profile
- `reparse.py` — extracts ground-truth titles from "Gemini\n<title>\nUpgrade" page headers, dedupes by URL, reclassifies with distribution-specific patterns

Key calibration rule: before writing any study guide, explicitly ask the student "how much can you solve cold vs. with AI help?" Polished HW submissions overestimate capability. Andrew's middle-position framing (exposure + pattern recognition, not fluency; 14-18hr budget) worked after two wrong calibrations.

Generalizes beyond STAT 410 — works for any course with a formula sheet, HW PDFs, and lecture material trapped in a chat app with no export API.

---

## PHIL 245 WORKFLOW PATTERN ANALYSIS

### 1. PER-CONVERSATION BREAKDOWN

**CONVERSATION 1: b4a824d5-62bf-4987 (short ID: b4a8)**

**Documents Uploaded:**
- `PHIL 245 Presentation Outline-2.pdf` (the core course material: an outline of the student's group's philosophy project on "The Ideal State")

**Initial Ask (verbatim):**
"I need a slideshow for this. The presentation has to last 10 minutes. I want many slides with short bullets per slide to keep the presentation engaging but also to remind me on all the points in the document. i'm familiar with the topics but i don't want to have to memorize everything. please ask me some questions to brainstorm how we'll design this presentation so that you can execute and create the powerpoint properly"

**Output Document Type:** PowerPoint presentation (.pptx file)

**Follow-up Steers/Corrections:**
- Requested brainstorming *before* execution (explicit design handshake phase)
- Constraints on style: "clean professional academic look," title slide editable for three presenter names
- Narrative requirement: must follow the outline's documented journey (not reorder)
- No audience interaction needed
- Text sizing feedback multiple times: "make the text bigger without making the images smaller," "bigger bigger bigger," "increase vertically, decrease horizontally" 
- Image handling: first wanted descriptive placeholders ("specify what each image should be at high-level"), then switched to pulling real images from Wikimedia Commons, then reverted to removing all images and restoring text-box placeholders
- Final cut: removed 10-15 redundant slides (e.g., "Laws and enforcement" section)

**Final Output:** 39-slide PowerPoint PPTX file, markdown-like structured outline visible in file (showed `--- Slide X ---` format in final review)

**Voice/Constraints:**
- Professional academic aesthetic (navy/teal color scheme hinted in design)
- Speaker notes emphasis over audience engagement ("reminders," not explanation)
- Explicitly mirrored the document's structure (journey-based, not reordered)
- Pragmatic about perfection: willing to revise aggressively

---

**CONVERSATION 2: d05b3502-41ed-49d6-87d2 (short ID: d05b)**

**Documents Uploaded:**
- `PHIL\ 245\ The\ Ideal\ State-2.docx` (group's working document on ideal governance system)
- `the\ ideal\ state\ brain\ dump.pdf` (student's personal rough notes/thinking process)

**Initial Ask (verbatim fragment):**
"so my group and I talked this over... Please just ask me some questions. Lead me through this thought process so that we can get to a point where we have a specific ideal state down in this document. It's not going to be perfect; it doesn't have to be perfect, but it has to be specific."

**Output Document Type:** Structured outline document (Word .docx), showing thought process + alternatives + reasoning (not just final decisions)

**Follow-up Steers/Corrections:**
- Rejected passive "listing final decisions"; insisted on showing *reasoning and journey*
- Major cuts: scrap Noble Lie section entirely, rethink bureaucracy section
- Constraint on voting test: must be objective/factual (not opinion-based)
- Specificity required on governance mechanics (age limits, party restrictions, bureaucracy structure)
- Final reframe: "what I actually want you to do is make a new document... listing the thought process, the alternatives that we considered, and the reasoning as well"
- Emphasis: "walking through my thought process so that the audience isn't just getting shit put on their plate without seeing where the shit came from"

**Final Output:** Structured document with multi-section organization:
1. Starting from First Principles (showing principle rejection/iteration)
2. The Governance Paradox (competency gap, revolution threat)
3. Political Authority, Obligation & Superiority
4. Counterarguments
Each section explicitly showed "roads not taken"

**Voice/Constraints:**
- Pedagogical/explanatory emphasis ("walking through thought process")
- Factual/transparent (no "inner circle" opaqueness)
- Pragmatic philosophy (willing to admit imperfection if specific)
- First-principles driven (test reasoning from institutional logic, not credentials)

---

**CONVERSATION 3: ab234838-d219-499d-9145-d9a2f (short ID: ab23)**

**Documents Uploaded:** None (this is the meta-request asking me to analyze the pattern)

**Initial Ask (verbatim):**
"look through my conversation history and find the ones for phil 245 where i upload some documents and ask you to make me a document based on those and then analyze the workflow i ask you to do, it's pretty much the same each time, and then /skillify that workflow"

**Output Document Type:** (Not applicable—this is the analysis request itself)

**Follow-up Steers/Corrections:** N/A

**Final Output:** N/A

---

### 2. CROSS-CONVERSATION PATTERN SYNTHESIS

**Recurring Shape of the Ask:**
"I have course material (PDF outline, working doc, brain dump, readings). I want you to transform it into a *different format* that serves a specific pedagogical/communication purpose. But don't just repackage—show me your reasoning first, iterate with me on design choices, and capture the *thinking process*, not just the final output."

**Always-Present Inputs:**
1. Course material in PDF or DOCX form (outlines, rough notes, assignments)
2. An explicit purpose (PowerPoint for presentation, outline for essay, hypothetically reading notes for studying)
3. A constraint on *how* the output should be structured (narrative flow, bullet-point density, reasoning visibility)
4. A request for **collaborative design before execution** (ask questions, brainstorm, clarify constraints—don't just build)

**Output Structure That Tends to Emerge:**
- Multi-section hierarchical organization (numbered sections, subsections with reasoning)
- Heavy use of markdown-like markers (`**bold**`, `---`, `##` headings, bullet lists)
- Explicit "alternatives considered" / "roads not taken" sections (pedagogical transparency)
- Final deliverable is a file path to an output document (PPTX, DOCX, or implied MD)

**What He Corrects/Pushes Back On (Non-Negotiable Rules):**
1. **Don't over-interpret the source material.** "We revised it a lot" (d05b); "ask me questions before you execute" (b4a8). He wants to co-author, not be written for.
2. **Show the reasoning, not just the answer.** Rejected simple "final decisions" outline; insisted on thought process and alternatives.
3. **Structure must match document intent, not AI preference.** "follow the same journey otherwise we prob won't reach 10 minutes" (b4a8); "the presentation outline was specifically designed" to follow a path.
4. **Pragmatism over perfection.** "It doesn't have to be perfect, but it has to be specific" (d05b). Accept constraints, don't gold-plate.
5. **Design by dialogue.** All three workflows began with clarifying questions before output generation—never just "here's your document."
6. **Mirror the student's voice and framing.** He notices pedantic language ("shit put on their plate"), rejects corporate-speak, expects academic-professional tone.

**Tooling Pattern:**
- Files saved to `~/Downloads/PHIL\ 245\ ...` (consistent naming)
- Outputs are always deliverable formats (PPTX, DOCX, implicit markdown)
- No intermediate artifacts; each conversation produces one final output file
- File versioning (`-2.pdf`, `-2.docx`) suggests iterative refinement, but he manages versions, not me

---

### 3. SKILLIFY-READINESS SUMMARY

**Trigger Phrase:**
"I have [course material] and I need [output format] for [purpose]. Help me design it first, then build it."

**Deterministic Steps:**
1. **Intake & Clarification** (non-skippable)
   - Identify the source material (PDF, DOCX, images, transcripts)
   - Identify the target format and purpose (PowerPoint, essay outline, study guide, etc.)
   - Ask 3–5 design questions before building (style, narrative structure, audience, constraints)
   - Confirm constraints (size, tone, specificity level)

2. **Brainstorm Collaboratively** (don't move to step 3 without explicit buy-in)
   - Propose design options (e.g., "3 slide layout options")
   - Get feedback on approach before touching the actual output file
   - Adjust scope based on feedback

3. **Draft & Iterate**
   - Generate initial output (PowerPoint, outline, etc.)
   - Student reviews; iterate on structure, sizing, content cuts, emphasis
   - Expect 2–4 rounds of refinement

4. **Finalize & Deliver**
   - Save to user's specified location (`~/Downloads/PHIL 245 ...`)
   - Confirm final file path

**Judgment Calls:**
- **When to ask more questions:** If source material is ambiguous (rough notes) or scope is undefined (what should the output *actually* do?), ask before guessing.
- **When to cut content:** If student says "it's redundant" or "cuts were made elsewhere," aggressively prune—don't defend every bullet.
- **When to restore vs. remove images:** If he says "placeholders, not real images," listen to that; pragmatism > polish.
- **How much reasoning to show:** Show *enough* that the audience sees the decision tree, but don't over-explain the philosophy (he's familiar with it).

**The Gotchas:**
1. **He will re-specify mid-project.** "Actually, I want X instead of Y" is normal; don't treat it as failure. (Image insertion, then removal; simple outline, then thought-process outline.)
2. **He values speed + pragmatism over perfection.** Don't offer multiple versions or "polish" passes unless asked.
3. **His written voice is casual but his output must be academic.** (Uses "shit," informal phrasing in notes, but final documents are formal.)
4. **Scope creep is his, not yours.** He'll add sections ("counterarguments," "bureaucracy rethink"), but he decides the boundary—ask "is this in scope?" if unsure.
5. **File versioning is implicit.** He manages `-2.pdf`, `-2.docx` in his Downloads; don't create new versions unless explicitly asked.
6. **The "thought process" is load-bearing.** For essay/outline outputs, showing alternatives isn't nice-to-have; it's the entire point. "Roads not taken" must be visible.

---

### TRIGGER + EXECUTION TEMPLATE FOR /SKILLIFY

**Skill Name:** `PHIL 245: Transform Course Material to Presentation/Outline`

**Trigger:** User uploads course PDFs/DOCXs + specifies output format (PowerPoint / essay outline / study guide) + purpose

**Core Loop:**
1. List source documents and confirm understanding
2. Ask 3 design questions (style, scope, narrative structure)
3. Propose outline/structure (don't build yet)
4. Get approval
5. Build output (PPTX via python-pptx or DOCX via python-docx)
6. Iterate on feedback (cuts, text sizing, section reordering)
7. Save to ~/Downloads with version bumping
8. Confirm file path

**Key Constraints to Encode:**
- Always ask before building
- Show reasoning for outlines (never just "final decisions")
- Mirror the student's narrative structure (don't reorganize)
- Aggressive pruning when asked (don't negotiate cuts)
- Academic voice + casual input tolerance
- Pragmatism: "specific > perfect"

---

That's the pattern. Three PHIL 245 workflows, all following a "upload course material → collaborative design → transform to presentation/outline format" shape, with a strong emphasis on reasoning visibility, pragmatic iteration, and respecting the student's original structure.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/andrewallen/.claude/skills/skillify

# Skillify

## Overview

Captures the workflow we just completed and packages it as a `SKILL.md` saved to `~/.claude/skills/<name>/`. The user owns the workflow; you own its translation into a durable, opinionated skill file.

## Principles

### Inventory before interviewing

**Read the conversation first, then ask.** Scan for: rules the user surfaced (corrections + validations), sources consulted, artifacts touched, anti-patterns hit, workflow steps in order, and before/after examples worth preserving. Most of what belongs in the skill is already in the conversation — asking about things you can infer wastes the user's time.

### Distinguish durable rules from one-off choices

**Not every decision belongs in the skill.** A one-off choice ("use 2.8M because the file has 2.86M rows") is not a rule. A durable rule is what generalizes ("verify every quantitative claim against source"). Only durable rules go in the skill body. One-off choices live inside worked examples.

### Anti-patterns are load-bearing

**Capture what NOT to do, not just what to do.** Pushback is signal. If the user said "this is a nothing burger" or "don't pad with vanity counts," that's a rule worth bottling. Most skills omit failure modes; ours shouldn't.

### Concrete examples next to abstract patterns

**Always include both.** Abstract rules without examples don't transfer. Pull 1–2 before/after pairs directly from the source session and embed them.

### Offer options, don't dictate

**Generated skills should present user choices, not edicts.** If the workflow involves iteration on subjective output (bullets, prose, code), bake "offer 2–3 options with one recommended pick" into the generated skill.

### Verify before recommending

**If a generated skill references files, paths, numbers, or external data, the skill must instruct verification.** Tell the generated skill to read source files and re-check counts.

## The Cycle

1. **Inventory** — scan the current conversation. Output a brief summary: rules surfaced, sources consulted, artifacts touched, anti-patterns hit, workflow steps observed, examples worth preserving. No questions yet.
2. **Interview** — ask only what you genuinely cannot infer. Likely gaps: skill name (propose 2–3 candidates), invocation triggers, anchor files/URLs, scope (project-specific vs general). Skip questions whose answers are obvious.
3. **Draft** — produce a `SKILL.md` following the template below. Show it before saving.
4. **Iterate** — accept revisions; user picks from options where applicable.
5. **Install** — write the final draft to `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. Confirm the path.

## Generated-skill template

```
---
name: <name>
description: Use when <trigger condition>
---

# <Title>

## Overview
## Anchor sources       (files/URLs/repos to read at start, if any)
## Inputs               (what user provides at invocation, if any)
## Principles           (bold lead + paragraph)
## The Cycle / Workflow (ordered steps)
## Hard Rules
## Anti-patterns        (table or list)
## Examples             (1–2 before/after pairs from the source session)
```

Use only the sections the workflow demands.

## Hard Rules

- **No drafting before inventory.** Skipping this step misses durable rules.
- **No question the conversation already answered.** Re-read first.
- **No skill without anti-patterns.** If the session had pushback, capture it; if not, ask the user for failure modes to avoid.
- **No skill without at least one concrete example.** Abstract rules alone don't transfer.

## Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Fix |
|---|---|
| Transcribing the session verbatim | Distill — keep durable rules, drop one-offs |
| Asking the user for things in the conversation | Read first, ask only the gaps |
| Skipping anti-patterns because "it went well" | Pushback is signal — capture every correction |
| Single-take output with no choice | Present 2–3 options where iteration matters |
| Vanity-count rules ("must do X 5 times") | Rules are about kind, not count |

input.prompt:
I'm packaging a Claude skill that captures a workflow Andrew has done multiple times for PHIL 245. The workflow: Andrew uploads philosophy reading PDFs and asks for a single short document (~5 pages) with key quotes from the source texts plus plain-English explanations of each.

Two conversations contain this workflow. I need a tight, structured extraction.

**Conversations:**
- `/Users/andrewallen/.claude/projects/-Users-andrewallen/1ada1e73-af2c-43fd-ab98-44292b8e82f9.jsonl` (Communist Manifesto + 1844 Manuscripts)
- `/Users/andrewallen/.claude/projects/-Users-andrewallen/a506e637-bd1d-4381-a947-f358d6f35292.jsonl` (Hayek + Mill + Mises selections)

JSONL format: each line is a record with a `message` field containing role + content.

**For each conversation, give me:**

1. **Initial ask, verbatim** (the first substantive user prompt — quote it in full, even if long)
2. **Source PDFs uploaded** (filenames + course context if visible)
3. **Output format requested:**
   - File type (PDF? DOCX? MD?)
   - Page/length limit
   - Per-quote structure (what fields go with each quote — context? plain-English translation? page reference? both?)
   - Ordering (chronological by source text? grouped by theme?)
4. **Constraints/rules surfaced through pushback or follow-ups:**
   - Anti-hallucination concerns ("how do I verify these quotes are real" — quote his exact words)
   - Length policing (did he say "too long"? cut anything?)
   - Tone/voice for the plain-English explanation (academic? casual? assume what level of background?)
   - Anything about quote selection (most important? specific themes? what counts as "key"?)
5. **Where the output got saved** (file path / directory)
6. **Tools/methods Claude used** (PDF text extraction? Just reading? python-docx? Anything notable.)
7. **Any explicit do-NOTs** Andrew stated

**Then a synthesis** (under 250 words) of what's invariant across both — that's the skill content. Especially:
- The verification/trust mechanism (he flagged hallucination risk in one of them — what did he end up using?)
- The exact output template (it's likely the same shape both times: quote → context → plain-English)
- Implicit rules from how Claude got corrected

Don't pad. Just extract.

## Draft `SKILL.md`

```markdown
---
name: reading-companion
description: Use when the user uploads philosophy/humanities reading PDFs and asks for a short companion document with key quotes plus plain-English explanations. Common pattern for PHIL 245 and similar reading-heavy courses.
---

# Reading Companion

## Overview

User uploads 2–3 dense reading PDFs (philosophy, theory, primary texts) and wants ONE short companion document: key quotes in order of appearance, each paired with a plain-English restatement and a "bigger picture" explanation. Output is a study aid, not a summary.

## Inputs

- 2–3 PDF paths, usually in `~/Downloads/`
- Optional: explicit page cap (default **5 pages**) and output format (default markdown; PDF if requested)

## The Cycle

1. **Read every source PDF in full.** Do not skim. Quote selection requires understanding the whole argument.
2. **Pick "key" quotes.** "Key" = thematically central to the author's argument, not just memorable. Distribute roughly evenly across the source texts. Order chronologically by appearance within each text; group by source/author.
3. **Draft the companion document** using the per-quote template (below).
4. **Police the page cap by cutting quotes, never by shrinking explanations.** If 5 pages is the limit and 14 quotes overflow, drop to 12 — don't compress the Plain English / Bigger Picture sections to fit.
5. **Append a Verification section** with searchable online links (see "Verification" below) — automatic, every time, without being asked.
6. **Save to `~/Downloads/`** with a descriptive filename like `Key_Quotes_<Authors>.md`. Do not version-bump existing files.

## Per-Quote Template

```
### [Short thematic label]

> "[Exact quote, verbatim from source]"

**Plain English:** [Modern, conversational restatement. Assume zero prior familiarity with the text. No jargon.]

**Bigger Picture:** [Why this quote matters in the author's broader argument. 1–3 sentences.]
```

Group quotes under `## <Author / Work>` headers. Within each section, order by appearance in the text.

## Verification (automatic — always include)

The user has explicitly flagged quote hallucination as a concern. To address it, every output ends with a **"Verify these quotes"** section containing:

- A link to a searchable online version of each source text (Project Gutenberg, Marxists.org, Econlib, Library of Economics and Liberty, Internet Archive, etc.)
- A one-line instruction: "Ctrl+F a distinctive 4–6 word phrase from any quote to confirm it's verbatim."

When pulling quotes, prefer distinctive multi-word phrases — they fail gracefully if invented (a fake quote won't survive a Ctrl+F against the real text). Quotes that are short or generic ("the ruling ideas of each age") are harder to verify; pair them with a more distinctive flanking sentence when possible.

## Hard Rules

- **5 pages is a hard cap, not a target.** Default page limit is 5 unless the user states otherwise. Do not exceed it. If quotes don't fit, cut quotes — never abbreviate the Plain English or Bigger Picture sections.
- **Every quote must be verbatim.** Copy from the source PDF text; do not paraphrase, modernize spelling, or "clean up" the original.
- **Chronological order within each source.** No thematic re-clustering.
- **No prior-knowledge assumed in Plain English.** Translate as if the reader has never heard of the author.
- **Save to `~/Downloads/`.** Don't write elsewhere or rename existing files.
- **No summary intro/outro.** The document is the quotes + explanations + verification links. Skip preambles like "This guide covers…"

## Anti-patterns

| Don't | Why |
|---|---|
| Stretch a 5-page limit to 6–7 pages | Hard cap. Cut quotes instead. |
| Cluster quotes by theme across authors | User wants chronological-within-source ordering. |
| Paraphrase quotes silently | Hallucination risk; user must be able to verify verbatim. |
| Skip the verification links section | Standing concern. Always include even when not asked. |
| Add an executive summary or conclusion | Document is reference material, not an essay. |
| Use academic jargon in Plain English | Defeats the purpose of "Plain English." |
| Output without reading the full PDFs | Quote selection requires understanding the whole argument. |

## Examples

**Source session:** Hayek + Mill + Mises (5-page MD)

> User: "Retrieve the most important quotes from each one of these and put them into a separate document. for each quote, translate it to plain simple modern english and also provide the bigger picture surrounding the quote. this separate document should be no longer than 5 pages so keep that in mind when accumulating quotes."

Output: `~/Downloads/Key_Quotes_Hayek_Mill_Mises.md` — 14 quotes (4 Hayek, 4 Mill, 5 Mises) + verification section with Econlib / Project Gutenberg / Library of Liberty links.

**Source session:** Communist Manifesto + 1844 Manuscripts (longer cap, 8–10 pages PDF)

> User: "Compile another document which, in the order that they appear in the text, shows each highlighted quote. For each quote in your second compiled document, provide a bit of context so that it makes sense. Do this by first providing a simple translation of it into modern conversational English, and then concisely connect each quote to the bigger picture."

Output: `~/Downloads/Marx_Key_Quotes_Guide.pdf` — 25 quotes (13 Manifesto, 12 Manuscripts), chronological within each work, Plain English + Bigger Picture per quote. Page-cap explicitly raised to 8–10 in this case; the 5-page default is overridden only by explicit user request.
```
